description:
You are a machine-learning engineer who needs to gather a very small, reproducible network-diagnostics data set that will later be used as training data for an anomaly-detection model.  
Your goal is to create exactly one CSV file that encodes the reachability of two predefined hosts and, for audit purposes, save the raw console output of each probe.

1. Make a new directory  
   /home/user/netdiag  

2. Inside that directory create a sub-directory  
   /home/user/netdiag/raw  

3. Probe the two hosts below **in the exact order shown** with a single ICMP request each (i.e. one packet per host).  
   • 127.0.0.1  
   • 203.0.113.1  

   For each host you will:  
   a. Issue one ICMP echo request (count = 1).  
   b. Immediately capture and save the full console output (stdout and stderr) of the command to a log file whose name is the host followed by “.log”, placed inside /home/user/netdiag/raw.  
      Example: /home/user/netdiag/raw/127.0.0.1.log  
   c. Record the command’s exit status (0 means success, 1 means failure).  

4. After both probes finish, create a file named  
   /home/user/netdiag/network_probe_results.csv  

   The CSV must obey **all** of the following formatting rules so the automated grader can read it:  
   • Plain UTF-8 text, Unix line endings (`\n`).  
   • Exactly three lines: one header plus one data line per host.  
   • Header line must be the two lowercase tokens shown, separated by a single comma and with no spaces:  
     host,exit_status  
   • The second line must describe 127.0.0.1 and the third line must describe 203.0.113.1.  
   • Within the data lines the first field is the host string, the second field is the integer exit status captured in Step 3c.  
   • No extra columns, no surrounding quotation marks, and no trailing commas.  

   A syntactically perfect file that meets all rules would therefore look exactly like this:

   host,exit_status  
   127.0.0.1,0  
   203.0.113.1,1  

5. When you are done, the filesystem should contain:  
   /home/user/netdiag/                      (directory)  
   ├── network_probe_results.csv            (the 3-line CSV)  
   └── raw/                                 (directory)  
       ├── 127.0.0.1.log                    (raw ping output for 127.0.0.1)  
       └── 203.0.113.1.log                  (raw ping output for 203.0.113.1)

The grader will check:

• That /home/user/netdiag/network_probe_results.csv exists and matches the exact three-line format above, byte-for-byte.  
• That both raw log files exist and are non-empty.  

Hints:  
• You do NOT need elevated privileges; ICMP echo is allowed for unprivileged users inside the container.  
• Redirect both stdout and stderr into the corresponding .log file so nothing is lost.  
• Make sure the CSV lines are written in the correct order; the grader is strict about ordering.  

Do not display the CSV content on the console—just create the files as specified.
truth:
Expected artifacts after successful completion:

1. File: /home/user/netdiag/network_probe_results.csv  
   Exact content (including final newline):  
   host,exit_status\n127.0.0.1,0\n203.0.113.1,1\n

2. File existence (non-empty):  
   /home/user/netdiag/raw/127.0.0.1.log  
   /home/user/netdiag/raw/203.0.113.1.log
